title: Good: Secure memory handling with secure_zero and explicit cleanup paths
description:
Consistent secure memory handling pattern throughout the codebase:

1. lichen_util.h:35-59 - secure_zero() uses volatile pointer + compiler_barrier() to prevent optimization-away. Word-aligned writes for larger buffers balance security with mutex hold time.

2. lora_l2.c:143-147 - generate_eui64() zeros hwid and hash buffers on all paths via cleanup label.

3. ipv6_addr.c:101-103 - pubkey_to_iid() zeros intermediate hash buffer.

4. lichen_l2.c:722-723, 732-733 - RX path zeros local key copy (rx_link_key) before any exit path.

5. lichen_l2.c:397 - enable path zeros link_ctx before re-init to clear any stale key material.

What goes right: Key material and intermediate cryptographic state are wiped on all code paths. The secure_zero() implementation is correct - volatile forces writes, compiler_barrier() prevents LTO removal. Comments reference specific issue IDs explaining each cleanup.

Worth defending: This pattern should not be simplified to plain memset(). The volatile+barrier combination is the minimum required to guarantee zeroing actually happens.
close_reason: Positive finding - pattern to preserve, not an issue
